coredata.dc:title: Effects and mechanism of dual-frequency power ultrasound on the molecular weight distribution of corn gluten meal hydrolysates
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Jian
Ma, Haile
Wang, Bei
Yagoub, Abu El-Gasim A.
Wang, Kai
He, Ronghai
Zhou, Cunshan
coredata.dc:description: The impact of dual-frequency power ultrasound (DPU) on the molecular weight distribution (MWD) of corn gluten meal (CGM) hydrolysates and its mechanism were investigated in the present study. The mechanism was studied from aspects of structural and nano-mechanical characteristics of the major protein fractions of CGM, viz. zein and glutelin. The results of molecular weight distribution indicated that DPU pretreatment of CGM was beneficial to the preparation of peptides with molecular weights of 200–1000Da. Moreover, FTIR spectral analysis and atomic force microscopy characterization showed that the DPU pretreatment changed the contents of secondary structure of proteins, decreased the particle height and surface roughness of glutelin, reduced the Young’s modulus and stiffness of zein while increased its adhesion force. In conclusion, DPU pretreatment of proteins before proteolysis is an efficient alternative method to produce short-chain peptides because of its positive effects originating from acoustic cavitation on the molecular conformation, nano-structures and nano-mechanical properties of proteins as well.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-frequency power ultrasound
Corn gluten meal
Molecular weight distribution
FTIR spectra
Atomic force microscopy
Nano-mechanical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dual-frequency power ultrasound pretreatment of CGM
Effects of DPU pretreatment on the molecular weight distribution of CGM hydrolysates
Effects of DPU pretreatment on the structural characteristics of CGM proteins
Effects of DPU pretreatment on the secondary structure of zein and glutelin predicted from FT-IR spectra
Effects of DPU pretreatment on the nano-structure of zein and glutelin
Effects of ultrasound on the nano-mechanical properties of CGM proteins

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The energy-gathered multi-frequency power ultrasound equipment and its functional modes. (a) Ultrasonic equipment, (b) sequential mode, (c) simultaneous mode.

The elution curves of corn gluten meal hydrolysates came from DPU pretreatment and the control. (Sonication parameters: 20kHz ultrasound pulsed on-time10s, 40kHz ultrasound pulsed on-time 5s, protein concentration of 40g/L, temperature of 30°C, power density of 400W/L, and duration of 15min.).

Effects of DPU treatment on the FTIR spectra of glutelin and zein. (Sonication parameters: 20kHz ultrasound pulsed on-time10s, 40kHz ultrasound pulsed on-time 5s, protein concentration of 40g/L, temperature of 30°C, power density of 400W/L, and duration of 15min.).
Decomposition of the FT-IR spectra of glutelin and zein.

Effects of DPU pretreatment on the nanostructure of glutelin and zein. (Sonication parameters: 20kHz ultrasound pulsed on-time10s, 40kHz ultrasound pulsed on-time 5s, protein concentration of 40g/L, temperature of 30°C, power density of 400W/L, and duration of 15min.).

Effects of DPU pretreatment on the height distribution of glutelin and zein. (Sonication parameters: 20kHz ultrasound pulsed on-time10s, 40kHz ultrasound pulsed on-time 5s, protein concentration of 40g/L, temperature of 30°C, power density of 400W/L, and duration of 15min.).
Molecular weight distribution of corn gluten meal hydrolysates from the control and dual-frequency power ultrasound (DPU).
Deconvoluted amide I and assignments of the secondary structure content (%).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The data in the parentheses represent change in the secondary structure content.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Effects of ultrasound pretreatment on the surface roughness and mechanical properties of zein and glutelin
∗
.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Within a row, means (n =20) with different superscript letters (a and b) indicated significant difference (P< 0.05.); NG, not given.

head.ce:title: Effects and mechanism of dual-frequency power ultrasound on the molecular weight distribution of corn gluten meal hydrolysates

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Science and Engineering
Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faulty of Agriculture, University of Zalingei, PO Box 6, Zalingei, Sudan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The impact of dual-frequency power ultrasound (DPU) on the molecular weight distribution (MWD) of corn gluten meal (CGM) hydrolysates and its mechanism were investigated in the present study. The mechanism was studied from aspects of structural and nano-mechanical characteristics of the major protein fractions of CGM, viz. zein and glutelin. The results of molecular weight distribution indicated that DPU pretreatment of CGM was beneficial to the preparation of peptides with molecular weights of 200–1000Da. Moreover, FTIR spectral analysis and atomic force microscopy characterization showed that the DPU pretreatment changed the contents of secondary structure of proteins, decreased the particle height and surface roughness of glutelin, reduced the Young’s modulus and stiffness of zein while increased its adhesion force. In conclusion, DPU pretreatment of proteins before proteolysis is an efficient alternative method to produce short-chain peptides because of its positive effects originating from acoustic cavitation on the molecular conformation, nano-structures and nano-mechanical properties of proteins as well.

ce:section.ce:para: Proteins are important components of the human diet and play an essential role in synthesis of structural and functional components of living systems. It is estimated that one fourth of the world population has protein deficiency. Unfortunately, a large number of protein resources, such as corn gluten meal (CGM), are underexploited. The CGM contains 60%–71% protein and whose major protein fractions are zein and glutelin, accounting for 68% and 28% of the total protein weight, respectively. The CGM protein is underexploited due to its peculiar composition. For instance, zein is water insoluble because it contains many hydrophobic amino acids which are buried inside the molecules, its deficiency in lysine and tryptophan, and thus has limited uses in human nutrition. It is reported that the bioavailability of CGM can be improved significantly by enzymatic modification [1], because the hydrolysates contain many small peptides, especially di- and tri-peptides, which can be absorbed more effectively than both intact protein and free amino acids (AAs) [2]. Moreover, ingestion of AAs residues from partial enzymatic hydrolysis of whole protein has beneficial effects on protein synthesis [3]. Nevertheless, the traditional enzymolysis has many drawbacks such as low conversion rate of the substrate, longer reaction time, and high energy consumption [4,5]. This is largely due to the unsuitable conformation of protein molecules, which makes it difficult for the enzyme to attack the protein bonds. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a more efficient enzymolysis method so as to unfold the protein molecules and overcome the technical drawbacks mentioned above.

The ultrasound technology, as an emerging non-thermal processing physical technology, has extensive applications in food system. Acoustic cavitation is regarded as the fundamental process responsible for the initiation of most of the sonochemical reactions in liquids [6,7], because the collapse of cavitation bubbles can generate violent physical forces (micro jets, shear forces, shock waves, etc.) and free radicals [8,9]. The ultrasonic frequency as well as the acoustic power is one of the major factors that influence the production and intensity of cavitation in liquids [10]. In this line, it was found that either dual-frequency or multi-frequency sonication was beneficial to enhancing the cavitation activity [11–13] by affecting the cavitation bubble temperature and/or the active cavitation bubble population. Recent years, the power ultrasound was widely introduced to improve the functional properties of proteins [14,15] and to produce bioactive peptides [16]. Also, it is reported that using power ultrasound to pretreat protein before enzymolysis can improve significantly the bioactivity of products, the reaction rate and the conversion rate of substrates [5,17,18]. Moreover, this treatment can change the secondary- and micro- structure of proteins, leading to exposure of more hydrolysis sites to be accessible to protease [16,19–21]. However, few systematic researches have focused on effects of dual-frequency power ultrasound on the molecular weight distribution of protein hydrolysates and on the nano-structural characteristic of ultrasound-facilitated enzymolysis of CGM.

Therefore, the objectives of the present work aimed to investigate the effect of dual-frequency power ultrasound (DPU) on the molecular weight distribution of CGM hydrolysates. Further, the mechanism was investigated from the points of changes in molecular conformation, nano-structural and nano-mechanical properties of major protein fractions of CGM.

Corn gluten meal (particle size of 0.21mm, crude protein content of 58.6%, and starch content of 15.4%) was obtained from Fenda starch Co. (Jiangsu, China). Alcalase 2.4L with an activity of 23 400U/mL was purchased from Novozymes Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All reagents used in the experiment were of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-frequency power ultrasound pretreatment of CGM
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the enzymolysis reaction, the CGM was pretreated by energy gathered multi-frequency power ultrasound (Fig. 1 a), which was developed by our team and manufactured by Five Pines Biotechnology Co. (Jiangsu Jiangda, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, China). The ultrasound was equipped with probes operating at five different frequencies (20, 28, 35, 40, 50kHz) and the maximum output acoustic power of each probe was 200W. In addition, this equipment has frequency modes of single frequency and dual frequencies. The latter can work both in sequential mode (Fig. 1b) and in simultaneous mode (Fig. 1c). An aliquot of 500mL CGM suspension with net protein content of 50gL−1 was put in the reaction vessel, the probes were submerged to a depth of 2.0cm in the solution and the temperature of the reaction solution was maintained constant by thermostat-controlled cycling water. The ultrasonic parameters were optimized by single factor experiment and as follows: sequential frequency mode 20kHz and 40kHz, temperature 30±2°C, 20kHz pulsed on-time10s during which 40kHz was pulsed off-time, 40kHz pulsed on-time 5s during which 20kHz was pulsed off-time, duration of 15min and power density of 400W/L. A control experiment was conducted, under the same conditions, using a magnetic stirring apparatus at a speed of 100rpm instead of ultrasound.

The pretreated suspensions of the CGM were diluted to a protein concentration of 20g/L. After 10min preheating at 50°C, the pH of the suspensions was adjusted to 9.0, and then enzyme (4750U/g) was added to initiate reaction. Throughout the enzymolysis time (60min), the pH of the reaction solution was maintained constant by continuous addition of 0.5M NaOH. At the end of enzymolysis, the mixtures were boiled for 10min to terminate the reaction after whose pH was adjusted to 7.0. Having cooled to room temperature, the hydrolysates were centrifuged at 5030× g for 15min, and the supernatants were collected and stored at 4°C for subsequent analysis.

The molecular weight distribution (MWD) of corn gluten meal hydrolysates (CGMHs) from both the control and DPU pretreatment were determined according to the method of our previous study [1]. The total surface area of the chromatogram was integrated and separated into three major peptide fractions, viz. fraction I with MW>1000, fraction II with MW 200–1000, and fraction III with MW<200Da. The relative content of each range was expressed as a percentage of the total area.

A 500mL of untreated (the control) and DPU pretreated CGM suspension (40g/L) under the best operation parameters were used to extract zein and glutelin according to the method described by Li [22] and Cheng [23] respectively, with some modification. In brief, the slurries were centrifuged at 5030× g for 15min, after which the precipitates were suspended in 300mL of 95% (v/v) ethanol and then incubated in a water bath at 55°C for 30min. The pH of the mixtures was adjusted to 9.0 with 4M NaOH, and the ethanol concentration was diluted to 60% (v/v) using deionized water. After standing for 30min at room temperature (25°C), the mixtures were centrifuged at 5030× g for 15min and supernatants and precipitates were obtained. Then glutelin was obtained from the alkali-solutions ofthe precipitates using anacid-isolation method. Zein was precipitated from the supernatants using 1% (w/v) NaCl solution. The precipitates of glutelin and zein were lyophilized using a freeze dryer (ALPHA 1–2, Martin Christ Inc., Osterode, Germany) and stored at 4°C for subsequent analysis.

The FT-IR spectra of zein (1.0mg/mL in 70% ethanol, v/v) and glutelin (0.05mg/mL in 0.01M phosphate buffer, pH 7.0) samples were scanned in the wavenumbers range from 400 to 4000cm−1 using a Nicolet 50 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Madison, WI, USA) with injection volume of 0.5mL and resolution of 4cm−1 at room temperature (25°C). The spectra were an average of 64 scans and the spectrum of a blank solution was subtracted from the average spectra. The data transformation, deconvolution and peak- separation analysis of Amide I band (1600–1700cm−1) were processed by OMNIC software (Thermo Scientific, Madison, WI, USA) and PeakFit Version 4.12 software (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA), respectively.

Atom force microscopy of zein and glutelin were performed using the method described by Guo et al. [24] with some modification. Zein solution of a concentration of 1mg/mL in 75% ethanol (v/v) and glutelin solution with the same concentration in 0.01M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) were prepared. A droplet of 5μL sample solution was deposited onto a freshly cleaved mica and dried in a petri dish at room temperature (25°C) for 4h. Topographic images and nano-mechanical properties of samples on mica were captured by a multimode atomic force microscope (Bruker Inc., Karlsruhe, Germany) in PeakForceQNM mode with a silicon cantilever. All the images were scanned in air using standard peak force-mode and a silicon cantilever with a spring constant of 19.96N/m, defection sensitivity of 64.77nm/v, and tip radius of 12.98nm. The scanning size for topographic images and nano-mechanical properties were 5μm×5μm and 2μm×2μm, respectively. Height distribution, surface roughness, Young’s modulus, stiffness and adhesion were obtained using the offline software Nanoscope Analysis 1.5 (Bruker Inc., Karlsruhe, Germany). Among these, the average Young’s modulus, average stiffness and average adhesion force were obtained by normalization processing of 512 data points in the region.

Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed and means were separated at the significance level of P <0.05. All analyses including calculations were conducted with the aid of the statistical software SPSS 18.0 (IBM Corporation, Armonk, NY, USA), the graphs were created in OriginPro 8.5 (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of DPU pretreatment on the molecular weight distribution of CGM hydrolysates
ce:section.ce:para: Molecular weight (MW) of peptides, which is one of the most important factors that influencing the quality of the hydrolysates, is highly correlate to their efficiency of absorption in the body as well as their bioactivities. The chromatograms of the molecular weight distribution of the peptides hydrolysates from both DPU and the control are depicted in Fig. 2 . The relative content, obtained from their peak areas relative to the total peak area, of each peptide fraction is presented in Table 1 . The fraction with MW<200 represents free amino acids, the fraction with MW 200–1000Da represents di- and tripeptide and oligopeptides, and the fraction with MW>1000Da represents polypeptides. By comparison, the DPU pretreatment made the fraction 200 –1 000Da higher than that of the control with an increase percentage of 30.3%, suggesting degradation of fractions of MW>1000Da into small-sized peptides during Alcalase-catalyzed hydrolysis of the sonicated CGM. Accordingly, the content of free amino acids (MW<200Da) and polypeptides (MW>1000Da) decreased 4.9% and 9.6%, respectively. This increase in the amounts of small peptide fractions in DPU pretreatment might be due to the exposure of the hydrophobic cores, which are buried inside the molecules of the protein, by ultrasound pretreatment [21]. On the other hand, the enzyme used (Alcalase) is a broad endoprotease, with a preference for sites containing hydrophobic residues [25]. Yang et al. [26] suggested that the ultrasound treatment might have inhibited the formation of peptides with a very low molecular weight to some extent. Zhu et al. [27] reported similar increase in peptides of small fractions after sonication.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of DPU pretreatment on the structural characteristics of CGM proteins
Effects of DPU pretreatment on the secondary structure of zein and glutelin predicted from FT-IR spectra
ce:section.ce:para: Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy is a molecular vibrational spectroscopic technique which can be used to determine the secondary structure content of a protein at any physical state [28]. This technique is based on the amide I region (1700∼1600cm−1) of a protein, which arises mainly from the CO stretching vibration with minor contributions from the out-of-phase CN stretching vibration [29]. The FT-IR spectra of glutelin and zein in the region of 2000∼1000cm−1 are shown in Fig. 3 a and b. As presented in these figures, there was no obvious difference between untreated and DPU pretreated zein in terms of the absorption region and the shape of the peaks, but the shape of DPU pretreated glutelin changed distinctly. After deconvolution and the overlapping components extraction procedures [30], the fitted peaks are shown in Fig. 4 . According to Refs. [28,30], the deconvoluted amide I bands and assignments of contents of the secondary structure of zein and glutelin are displayed in Table 2 . Compared to the control, the DPU pretreatment decreased the content of α-helix and β-turn in zein by 3.3% and 3.8% respectively, while increased β-sheet by 8.2% and random coil by 4.2%. However, as far as glutelin was considered, the DPU pretreatment decreased the β-turn by 27.2% while increased α-helix, β-sheet and random coil by 3.8%, 16.5% and 24.8% respectively. The changes in the secondary structure content might be due to the fact that the free radical, micro jets, shear forces, shock waves and turbulence which were induced by ultrasound have disrupted the interactions between the local sequences of amino acids and between the different parts of the protein molecule. Differences in estimations of the secondary structure between the CD spectral analysis and FT-IR spectral analysis are found [21], that is because CD is slightly better for helix conformation prediction while estimation of the contents of antiparallel β-sheet and other structures are more reliable when using FT-IR spectral analysis [31,32]. Liu et al. [33] utilized energy-gathered ultrasound to pretreat wheat germ protein and the results of FT-IR showed that the ultrasound increased the content of β-turn and decreased the content of β-sheet. These contradictory results, compared to our results, might arise from the differences in sonication conditions, such as the type of ultrasound used, the frequency mode, the power density as well as the intrinsic characteristics of the sonicated material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of DPU pretreatment on the nano-structure of zein and glutelin
ce:section.ce:para: It is generally accepted that the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) observation only can give an overview of a certain zone, but the atom force microscopy scanning (AFM) can provide more details of a single protein particle, such as three dimension height, particle size and surface roughness. The AFM images and height distribution of glutelin and zein are displayed in Figs. 5 and 6 , respectively. As can be seen from these figures, the height distribution of untreated guletilin was principally between 300 and 350nm, and the particle size was about 0.8μm (Fig. 5a and Fig. 6a). However, irradiations of DPU on glutelin resulted in fairly smooth surface of particles and mainly reduced the height distribution to below 20nm, with a little distribution found between 50 and 80nm. But it raised the particle size larger than that of the control (Fig. 5b and Fig. 6b). These results were in accordance with the results of our previous findings of SEM on zein and glutelin [21]. In regard to zein, the untreated zein particles aggregated together with an intensive arrangement (Fig. 5c). Guo et al. [24] found that the native zein was presented in the form of mesh structure with some particles aggregated. The present study did not show the mesh structure, this was possible due to the difference in scanning size. Having undergone the DPU pretreatment, however, the zein protein particles appeared sunken which was present as circular holes in the two-dimensional diagram. Moreover, these particles arranged quite orderly in the form of semi-ring which might be due to the fact that ultrasound might have destroyed the mesh structure of the protein (Fig. 5d). Additionally, particles aggregation and the height distribution became wider as observed in Fig. 6c and d. Ren [34] primarily discovered the self-assembling dynamic course of zein under the action of sweeping frequency and pulsed ultrasound with the AFM technology.

Mendez-Vilas et al. [35] indicated that surface roughness played an important role in the biomolecules adsorption. The surface roughness can be expressed as roughness average (Ra ) and root mean square of roughness (Rq ). The effect of DPU pretreatment on the surface roughness of zein and glutelin is shown in Table 3 . Irrespective of the type of the protein (zein or glutelin), the values of Rq are higher than those of Ra , which is mainly due to the fact that the procedures of determination of Rq and Ra are basically different. The surface roughness of the DPU pretreated zein and untreated zein showed no significant (P> 0.05) difference. This is possibly ascribed to the spherical or dumbbell-like shape of zein particles, giving strong rigidity and hence the particles acquire high resistance to the impacts of ultrasound waves. However, the surface roughness of DPU pretreated glutelin was significantly (P< 0.05) lower than that of the control, which ascertained the smoothness of the surface of DPU glutelin particles. According to the equation proposed by Bowen et al. [36], the lower the surface roughness, the higher the adsorption force. Therefore, it can be concluded that ultrasound pretreatment was beneficial for the adsorption between protein and Alcalase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of ultrasound on the nano-mechanical properties of CGM proteins
ce:section.ce:para: Structural change in a protein may lead to changes in nano-mechanical properties of protein particles. In addition to characterize the three-dimensional topography of biological macromolecules, the AFM also can be used to study the interaction force between biomolecules [37–39], the structural conformation of proteins [40] and the structure of starch [41]. Young’s modulus, also known as the tensile modulus, is one of the most common elastic modulus, which can be used to characterize the easiness to produce elastic deformation of solid materials. The higher the value, the greater the force needed to occur to a certain elasticity deformation, i.e. the greater the rigidity of the material. The Young’s modulus, stiffness and adhesion force of the DPU pretreated and untreated zein and glutelin are shown in Table 3. Under the condition of deformation at a level of 3nm, the values of both Young’s modulus and stiffness of zein were greater than those of glutelin, indicating higher resistance of zein to deformation than glutelin. These findings coincided with the results of microstructures of zein and glutelin obtained by SEM [21]. In respect to zein, the DPU pretreatment decreased the Young’s modulus from 38.2 to 32.3Gpa and reduced the stiffness from 208.4 to 171.5N/m while increased the adhesion force from 13.9 to 14.9nN, suggesting that the DPU treatment made the protein more flexible, which facilitated the adherence between proteins and enzymes, and hence led to an improvement of the enzymolysis process. On the contrary, the adhesion force of the DPU pretreated glutelin was much lower than that of the control, which might be largely associated with the particles height (<20nm). Moreover, very great values (not given) of Young’s modulus and stiffness of the DPU pretreated glutelin were found. This might be due to the fact that the height of the particles was such low as the cantilever probe was easy to stab the mica substrate surface, thus showed a larger elastic modulus and rigidity. These changes in nano-structure and nano-mechanical properties could be attributed to the formation of localized hot spots upon collapse of bubbles, and to the generation of microjets, shear forces, shock waves and turbulence [8,42,43] which were induced by the acoustic cavitation.

In conclusion, these changes in the secondary structure, nano-structure and nano-mechanical properties might lead to differences in binding property between enzyme and proteins. Meanwhile, these changes might have resulted in difference in cleavage sites because each enzyme has its preference sites, for example, alcalase can hydrolyze proteins with broad specificity for peptide bonds and has a preference for a large uncharged residue in P1, and thus the hydrolysates have different molecular weight. On the other hand, the DPU treatment made the protein more flexible and easier to be combined with the protease, therefore the fractions of MW>1000Da might have been degradated into small-sized peptides during hydrolysis, and thus the relative content of the fraction with MW 200–1000Da was higher than that of the control.

Dual frequency power ultrasound pretreatment was found advantageous to the formation of short-chain peptides with molecular weights of 200–1000Da. This pretreatment changed the relative content of the secondary structure (α-helix, β-chain and random coil) of zein and glutelin, decreased the height and surface roughness of glutelin, reduced the Young’s modulus and stiffness while increased the adhesion force of zein. In conclusion, ultrasound pretreatment of protein prior to proteolysis is a cost-effective and eco-friendly alternative method to produce peptides with small molecular weight.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their appreciation for the support obtained from Grant (2013AA102203) of the Project of National 863 Plan of China, National Natural Science Foundation of China (31471698), Key University Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province (12KJA550001), Natural Science Foundation of Southwest University of Science and Technology (15zx7143).
